Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Response To Requests For Production Of Documents From Defendant Owl Inc And For Sanctions
Off Calendar May-20-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. ALAN YEE, Esq. of Siegel, Yee, Brunner & Mehta (tel: 510-839-1200) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. MICHAEL WALKER, Esq. of Lewis Brisbois Bisgaard & Smith LLP (tel: 415-362-2580) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFFS KATHRYN KASCH, KYLE NEWPORT, AND MARIA NEWPORT's Motion To Compel Further Response To Requests For Production Of Documents From Defendant Owl Inc And For Sanctions.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Kathryn Ruth Kasch seeks documents from defendant Owl, Inc. that she contends are relevant to her alter ego allegations. Specifically, Kasch has alleged that Owl, Inc. is the alter ego of its owner, defendant Laster Walker. Owl has largely refused to produce documents responsive to Kasch's RFPs, citing privacy and relevance objections. It has permitted Kasch's counsel to view a financial document purportedly to show its corporate independence and has produced a heavily redacted set of corporate minutes. Owl has not substantiated any claim that responding to Kasch's discovery requests in full would subject it to particular burdens. (See Williams v. Superior Court (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 549 ["An objection based upon burden must be sustained by evidence showing the quantum of work required."] [internal quotation marks omitted].)

The court largely overrules Owl's objections. It orders Owl to produce documents responsive to RFP Nos. 78 and 79 but limits the response to documents "reflecting or comprising" the payments specified in the RFPs rather than documents "referring" or "relating to" the payments. It orders Owl to produce documents responsive to RFP No. 89 but again limits the response to documents "reflecting or comprising" the expenditures referred to in the RFP. It orders OWL to produce documents responsive to RFP No. 95.

The court denies Kasch's motion for further responses or production in response to RFP No. 92. 28 Trans LLC is, according to the operative complaint's allegations, a successor company created by Laster Walker, who used Owl as his alter ego. Only if Kasch can show facts that Walker in fact used Owl as his alter ego, and also treated 28 Trans as his alter ego, would this RFP call for relevant documents. Kasch has not met her burden here of articulating specific facts showing good cause justifying this discovery. See CCP 2031.310(b)(1).

Owl, Inc. may produce any documents it deems confidential subject to a protective order. The parties are ordered to meet and confer concerning an appropriate protective order within one week of entry of this order. Owl's further responses and production are due three weeks after entry of this order.

Kasch seeks sanctions. CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The court awards sanctions of $2000 to Kasch, payable by Owl within three weeks of entry of this order. The reduced sanctions amount reflects Kasch's degree of success with this motion and the court's conclusion that Owl's objections largely lacked substantial justification.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Response To Request For Production Of Documents From Defendant Laster Walker And For Sanctions
Off Calendar May-20-2025 Continued To Jul-07-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. ALAN YEE, Esq. of Siegel, Yee, Brunner & Mehta (tel: 510-839-1200) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. MICHAEL WALKER, Esq. of Lewis Brisbois Bisgaard & Smith LLP (tel: 415-362-2580) for Defendants.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFFS KATHRYN KASCH, KYLE NEWPORT, AND MARIA NEWPORT's Motion To Compel Further Response To Request For Production Of Documents From Defendant Laster Walker And For Sanctions.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling, modifying the new hearing date to July 7, 2025.

By companion order today, the court has largely granted plaintiff Kathryn Ruth Kasch's motion to compel further production of documents from Owl, Inc. The present motion concerns Kasch's request for documents from Laster Walker, an individual whom Kasch alleges uses Owl Inc. as his alter ego. The court recognizes that production by Walker implicates privacy concerns that production by Owl Inc. does not; that parties seeking to compel production must articulate specific facts showing good cause justifying this discovery (CCP 2031.310(b)(1)), but that a party should not have to be able to prove her case to be able to obtain discovery to prove her case. Alter ego liability is the exception, not the rule. The court's discovery order today is intended to provide Kasch with initial discovery and permit her to make a further showing of specific facts if supported.

As to the following RFPs, the court overrules Walker's objections and orders production, other than documents for which Walker claims privilege, and for which he must provide a privilege log: RFP Nos. 1, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12, and 31.

As to the following RFPs, the court continues motion for hearing to July 7, 2025: RFP Nos. 13, 14, 15, and 27. Kasch shall provide a supplemental brief of no more than five pages articulating specific facts justifying discovery by no later than nine court days in advance of the hearing. Walker may respond in a supplemental brief of no more than five pages by no later than five court days in advance of the hearing.

The court denies relief as to RFP Nos. 2 and 3. Kasch included these in her notice of motion as disputed RFPs but they do not appear in her separate statement. (See CRC 3.1345.)

Walker may produce any documents he deems confidential subject to a protective order. The parties are ordered to meet and confer concerning an appropriate protective order within one week of entry of this order. Walker's further responses and production are due three weeks after entry of this order.

The court puts over the issue of sanctions to the continued hearing. If the parties have resolved their discovery disputes, Kasch may request that the motion be withdrawn at any point before the continued hearing.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

ALLISON N. STEWART, Esq., LEWIS BRISBOIS BISGAARD & SMITH LLP 415.362.2580 Allison.Stewart@lewisbrisbois.com Attorneys for Defendants, TERRANCE HENRY,
LASTER B. WALKER and OWL, INC. (In-court)

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS OWL, INC., DBA OWL TRANSPORTATION, TERRENCE HENRY, LASTER WALKER'S DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT

Defendants' demurrer to the second amended complaint (SAC) is overruled. The SAC adequately pleads elder abuse under a physical abuse theory (W&I Code 15610.63). (SAC 9:14-22.) The first amended complaint (FAC) sought to plead elder abuse under a neglect theory (W&I Code 15610.57). (FAC 10:7-16.) Unlike the neglect theory, the physical abuse theory requires no "substantial caretaking or custodial relationship." (CACI 3103, 3106.) Winn v. Pioneer Medical Group Inc. (2016) 63 Cal.4th 148, 152 - a neglect case - is not contrary to California's approved jury instructions.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT OWL, INC., DBA OWL TRANSPORTATION, TERRENCE HENRY, LASTER WALKER MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT

Defendants' motion to strike punitive damages from the second amended complaint (SAC) is denied. The SAC pleads that defendants were transporting decedent to a doctor's appointment in a van. The driver recklessly accelerated from a stop, flipping decedent's unsecured wheelchair over and knocking him out. The van driver then dragged the decedent back into his wheelchair without immobilizing his spine, and lied to EMTs, saying decedent had a heart attack. Despite having a video of the accident, defendants "continued to conceal the truth." Decedent suffered a severe spine injury and died five days later. (SAC 3:27-6:27.) These allegations suffice to support punitive damages at the pleading stage.

order signed.

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-19-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances
ALAN S. YEE, ESQ., SIEGEL, YEE, BRUNNER & MEHTA (510) 839-1200 alanyee@siegelyee.com Attorneys for Plaintiffs KATHRYN RUTH KASCH, KYLE NEWPORT, and MARIA MERCEDES NEWPORT (in court)
MICHAEL A. WALKER, Esq., LEWIS BRISBOIS BISGAARD & SMITH LLP 415.362.2580 Michael.Walker@lewisbrisbois.com Attorney for Defendant OWL, INC.

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT OWL, INC., DBA OWL TRANSPORTATION MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT

Defendant's motion to strike is granted with 15 days leave to amend. Plaintiff alleges that defendant's "reckless actions" caused decedent's death. (Amended Complaint, 2:6.) The allegations of the amended complaint are conclusory and currently do not support a claim for punitive damages. (Grieves v. Superior Court (1984) 157 Cal.App.3d 159, 166 ["The mere allegation an intentional tort was committed is not sufficient to warrant an award of punitive damages. [citation] 971) 2, at pp. 9-10.) Not only must there be circumstances of oppression, fraud or malice, but facts must be alleged in the pleading to support such a claim."].) In fact, paragraph 17 of the amended complaint alleges that decedent "was injured in a manner currently unknown." This allegation suggest that plaintiff does not know how the accident occurred and cannot honestly allege that it was the result of fraud, oppression, or malice.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-19-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances
ALAN S. YEE, ESQ., SIEGEL, YEE, BRUNNER & MEHTA (510) 839-1200 alanyee@siegelyee.com Attorneys for Plaintiffs KATHRYN RUTH KASCH, KYLE NEWPORT, and MARIA MERCEDES NEWPORT (in court)
MICHAEL A. WALKER, Esq., LEWIS BRISBOIS BISGAARD & SMITH LLP 415.362.2580 Michael.Walker@lewisbrisbois.com Attorney for Defendant OWL, INC.

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT OWL, INC., DBA OWL TRANSPORTATION DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT

Defendant's demurrer to cause of action three [elder abuse] is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff fails to state a cause of action. (CCP 430.10(e).)

A plaintiff seeking heightened remedies under the elder abuse statute (Welfare & Institutions Code ("W&I") section 15657) must show physical abuse or neglect. W&I 15610.57 defines "neglect" and notes that it applies to persons "having the care or custody of an elder."

In Winn v. Pioneer Medical Group, Inc. (2016) 63 Cal.4th 148, 158, the court explains that the neglect definition contemplates "the existence of a robust caretaking or custodial relationship-that is, a relationship where a certain party has assumed a significant measure of responsibility for attending to one or more of an elder's basic needs that an able-bodied and fully competent adult would ordinarily be capable of managing without assistance." Here, providing transportation is not a "basic need" and even if it were, there are no alleged facts showing that defendant had a "robust caretaking" role for decedent. It is true that defendant has heightened duties as a common carrier. But every bus, taxi, Uber, or van driver does not enter into a "caretaking" role simply because an elder boards his or her vehicle. The driver relationship is a "temporally limited affiliation." "Appearing not only in section 15610.57 but also elsewhere in the Act, the phrase 'care or custody' evokes a bond that contrasts with a casual or temporally limited affiliation." (Winn, 63 Cal.4th at 161.)

People v. Morales (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1075 is an inapposite criminal case.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
